messages: Update: I just got an email from the incoming company — they're putting new hires on hold for the near term and have paused their moving assistance. They say they still want me, but can't confirm a firm start date or relocation support right now.
To complicate things, I already handed in my resignation yesterday; my last day is scheduled in four weeks. I'm suddenly wondering whether to try to retract it. What should I consider?
My manager has already started reallocating work and I've got a candidate in mind to train. If I try to stay, will that look unstable, or can it be handled professionally?
If I press the new employer to either let me start remotely or pay for the move cancellation, how should I phrase that without sounding confrontational?
We already booked a moving company and paid deposits on a rental; cancelling would be costly. How do I approach getting reimbursed for cancel fees given their pause on the package?
Should I go ahead and start any paperwork for benefits and payroll with the new employer in case they're able to onboard me remotely, or would that be premature?
My spouse had given notice at their job to focus on the move. Now they're in a bind. How do we coordinate next steps at home while waiting on the new employer?
From a career perspective, is it safer to stay put and try to rescind the resignation, or to insist the new employer honor the original timeline some other way?
If the company won't restore the relocation package but still wants me, is it reasonable to ask for an increased signing bonus to cover our moving losses?
I'm concerned about how this looks on my resume if I delay or never start—any ways to frame the timeline so it doesn't hurt long-term prospects?

I need an immediate, prioritized action plan for the next two weeks that assumes the offer is still live but onboarding and moving support are suspended. Start with what to do today, then week by week.

Also draft two concise scripts: one I can use to ask my current boss if I can withdraw my resignation without awkwardness, and another to send to incoming HR to request either a remote start or compensation for cancellation costs. Keep each under five sentences.

Give me a short checklist (no more than eight items) prioritizing: protecting cash/payments we've already made, preserving health and retirement coverage, and minimizing reputational risk with both employers.

Provide two quick negotiation lines to open and close conversations with the new employer: one focused on covering out-of-pocket move expenses and one focused on deferring physical relocation while starting remotely.
Give two brief lines for the current manager conversation if they push back on me staying—phrases that preserve goodwill and keep future options open.
Finally, recommend a conservative follow-up schedule to touch base with the incoming company over the next few months without seeming needy—how often and in what tone should I reach out?
